title: The Man Who Takes a Bica
summary: A Lisbon waiter and her long-time regular argue over whether years of familiarity let us see a person more truly or quietly stop us from seeing them at all.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: You have been coming to this cafe for eleven years, Senhor Okafor, and I realised this morning that I could not tell you a single thing about your life beyond your coffee order.

body.speaker: Tunde
body.text: Which is precisely why I keep coming. What you call not knowing me, I would call being spared. Out there I am explained to people before I open my mouth; here I am merely the man who takes a bica standing up.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: But does it not trouble you? We see each other more often than I see most of my friends, and yet I have, in a sense, stopped looking. You have become furniture to me, and I do not mean that unkindly.

body.speaker: Tunde
body.text: I take no offence. Furniture is loyal. The trouble, if there is one, is not that you have stopped looking but that you believe a longer look would have shown you more. Familiarity does not deepen the picture; it freezes it. A glance held a moment too long would have served us both better than a thousand pleasant mornings.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: That is a convenient theory for a man who likes his privacy. Surely the people who have watched us for years know things our new acquaintances never will. My grandmother could read my mood from the doorway.

body.speaker: Tunde
body.text: She could read the moods you had already shown her. What she could not see was the mood you had never dared show, because the version of you she had filed away left no room for it. The closer the file, the harder to revise.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: So you are saying that the people who recognise us are, in fact, the least equipped to notice when we change. That seems too neat, and a little cruel to the people who love us.

body.speaker: Tunde
body.text: Not cruel. Honest. Love and attention are not the same currency, though we spend them as if they were. I have been loved by people who had stopped attending to me years before, and attended to, beautifully, by strangers who felt nothing. I have watched widowers grieve a wife they had ceased, in any living sense, to perceive.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: Then what would you have me do, refuse to know anyone well? I cannot serve a thousand strangers and call it intimacy. There is something to be said for the person who notices you have ordered nothing today.

body.speaker: Tunde
body.text: There is everything to be said for it. I am not arguing against being known; I am arguing against the lazy faith that being known is automatic, that it accrues like interest simply because the years pass. It does not. It decays unless renewed.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: Renewed how? I cannot interview my regulars each morning to check whether they have become someone new overnight.

body.speaker: Tunde
body.text: No, but you could resist the small certainty that you already have my answer before I give it. The moment you finish my sentence for me, you have replaced me with your memory of me, and your memory, however affectionate, stopped updating long ago.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: I confess I do that. I had your bica poured before you reached the counter. I thought it was a kindness.

body.speaker: Tunde
body.text: It is a kindness, and it is also a small wall. Both at once. Most of what passes between familiar people is exactly that: a tenderness that doubles as a way of not having to look again. We mistake the comfort for closeness.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: You have given me an uncomfortable morning, Senhor Okafor. I shall have to start watching the people I am sure I already understand.

body.speaker: Tunde
body.text: Do not watch them harder. Watch them as if you might be wrong. That is the only recognition worth having, the kind that leaves the door open. And, since you are watching, I shall, today, take the bica sitting down.

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences for emphasis (What/It clefts)
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ (cleft) tách một thông tin ra để nhấn mạnh: 'What you call not knowing me, I would call being spared' và 'Which is precisely why I keep coming'. Cấu trúc 'What + mệnh đề, + động từ to be' đưa trọng tâm vào phần sau, một dấu hiệu của văn phong C1.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Cleft sentences split one idea to foreground it: 'What you call not knowing me, I would call being spared.' The 'What... is/would be...' pattern (and 'It is X that...') redistributes emphasis and signals controlled, argumentative prose.

grammarNotes.title: Concession with 'though' and 'however'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người nói C1 nhượng bộ trước khi phản bác: 'though we spend them as if they were' và 'your memory, however affectionate, stopped updating'. 'However + tính từ' chèn giữa câu thừa nhận một điểm đúng rồi vẫn giữ lập luận.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Skilled speakers concede before they counter: 'though we spend them as if they were' and 'your memory, however affectionate, stopped updating long ago'. Inserting 'however + adjective' grants a point while holding the line of argument.

grammarNotes.title: Comparative correlatives ('the + comparative, the + comparative')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'The closer the file, the harder to revise' liên kết hai mức độ tăng/giảm song song. Đây là cách diễn đạt cô đọng một quy luật, thường dùng trong văn nghị luận.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The closer the file, the harder to revise' uses the double comparative to lock two scaling quantities together — a compact way to state a rule, common in essayistic argument.

grammarNotes.title: 'as if' + past form for hypothetical manner
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'as if they were' và 'Watch them as if you might be wrong' dùng 'as if' để diễn tả một cách thức giả định, không có thật. Thì quá khứ sau 'as if' đánh dấu tính giả định.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'as if they were' and 'Watch them as if you might be wrong' use 'as if' to express a hypothetical, counter-to-fact manner; the past form after 'as if' marks the unreal mood.

translationVi:
Inês: Ông đã đến quán cà phê này mười một năm rồi, ông Okafor, và sáng nay tôi nhận ra rằng tôi không thể nói được một điều duy nhất nào về cuộc đời ông ngoài việc ông gọi cà phê.
Tunde: Đó chính xác là lý do tôi cứ tiếp tục đến. Cái mà cô gọi là không hiểu tôi, tôi lại gọi là được tha cho. Ở ngoài kia tôi bị người ta diễn giải trước cả khi tôi mở miệng; ở đây tôi chỉ đơn thuần là người uống một ly bica khi đang đứng.
Inês: Nhưng điều đó không làm ông bận tâm sao? Chúng ta gặp nhau thường xuyên hơn cả tôi gặp hầu hết bạn bè mình, vậy mà, theo một nghĩa nào đó, tôi đã ngừng quan sát. Ông đã trở thành đồ đạc quen thuộc với tôi, và tôi không có ý nói điều đó một cách ác ý.
Tunde: Tôi không thấy bị xúc phạm. Đồ đạc thì trung thành. Vấn đề, nếu có, không phải là cô đã ngừng quan sát mà là cô tin rằng nhìn lâu hơn thì sẽ thấy được nhiều hơn. Sự quen thuộc không làm bức tranh sâu sắc hơn; nó làm bức tranh đóng băng. Một cái nhìn nán lại quá một khoảnh khắc hẳn đã giúp cả hai ta nhiều hơn cả nghìn buổi sáng dễ chịu.
Inês: Đó là một lý thuyết tiện lợi cho một người thích sự riêng tư. Chắc chắn những người đã quan sát ta nhiều năm sẽ biết những điều mà người mới quen không bao giờ biết được. Bà tôi có thể đọc được tâm trạng tôi ngay từ ngưỡng cửa.
Tunde: Bà ấy có thể đọc được những tâm trạng mà cô đã từng thể hiện với bà. Cái bà không thể thấy là tâm trạng mà cô chưa bao giờ dám thể hiện, bởi vì phiên bản của cô mà bà đã lưu lại không chừa chỗ nào cho nó. Hồ sơ càng sát thì càng khó sửa lại.
Inês: Vậy ông đang nói rằng những người nhận ra chúng ta, trên thực tế, lại là những người ít có khả năng nhận ra nhất khi chúng ta thay đổi. Điều đó nghe quá gọn gàng, và hơi tàn nhẫn với những người yêu thương chúng ta.
Tunde: Không tàn nhẫn. Trung thực thôi. Tình yêu và sự chú ý không phải là cùng một loại tiền tệ, dù chúng ta tiêu chúng như thể chúng giống nhau. Tôi đã được yêu bởi những người đã ngừng quan tâm chú ý đến tôi từ nhiều năm trước, và được chăm chú một cách đẹp đẽ bởi những người lạ chẳng cảm thấy gì cả. Tôi đã chứng kiến những người góa vợ tiếc thương một người vợ mà họ đã thôi, theo bất cứ nghĩa sống động nào, cảm nhận được.
Inês: Vậy ông muốn tôi làm gì, từ chối hiểu rõ bất kỳ ai sao? Tôi không thể phục vụ một nghìn người lạ rồi gọi đó là sự thân mật. Vẫn có điều gì đó đáng nói về người nhận ra rằng hôm nay bạn chẳng gọi gì cả.
Tunde: Có rất nhiều điều đáng nói về điều đó. Tôi không phản đối việc được hiểu rõ; tôi phản đối niềm tin lười biếng rằng việc được hiểu là tự động, rằng nó tích lũy như lãi suất chỉ vì năm tháng trôi qua. Không phải vậy. Nó phân rã trừ khi được làm mới.
Inês: Làm mới bằng cách nào? Tôi không thể phỏng vấn những khách quen của mình mỗi sáng để kiểm tra xem họ có trở thành một người khác qua đêm hay không.
Tunde: Không, nhưng cô có thể chống lại sự chắc chắn nhỏ nhặt rằng cô đã biết câu trả lời của tôi trước khi tôi nói ra. Khoảnh khắc cô nói nốt câu thay cho tôi, cô đã thay thế tôi bằng ký ức của cô về tôi, và ký ức của cô, dù trìu mến đến đâu, đã ngừng cập nhật từ lâu.
Inês: Tôi thú nhận là tôi làm thế. Tôi đã rót bica cho ông trước cả khi ông đến quầy. Tôi tưởng đó là một sự tử tế.
Tunde: Đó là một sự tử tế, và cũng là một bức tường nhỏ. Cả hai cùng một lúc. Phần lớn những gì diễn ra giữa những người quen thuộc đúng là như vậy: một sự dịu dàng đồng thời cũng là một cách để không phải nhìn lại lần nữa. Chúng ta nhầm sự thoải mái với sự gần gũi.
Inês: Ông đã cho tôi một buổi sáng khó chịu, ông Okafor. Tôi sẽ phải bắt đầu quan sát những người mà tôi chắc rằng mình đã hiểu rõ.
Tunde: Đừng quan sát họ chăm chú hơn. Hãy quan sát họ như thể cô có thể đã sai. Đó là sự nhận biết duy nhất đáng có, loại để cánh cửa ngỏ. Và, vì cô đang quan sát, hôm nay tôi sẽ uống bica khi ngồi.

questions.prompt: What is Tunde's central claim about familiarity?
options: It steadily deepens our knowledge of others over time
It freezes an image of a person rather than enriching it
It is impossible between a waiter and a customer
It matters less than first impressions
questions.explanation: Tunde says 'Familiarity does not deepen the picture; it freezes it,' directly rejecting the idea that long acquaintance enriches knowledge.
questions.hint: Look at his reply that begins 'I take no offence.'

questions.prompt: Why does Tunde value the cafe specifically?
options: Because Inês knows his whole history
Because there he is not pre-explained to others
Because he prefers drinking standing up
questions.explanation: He contrasts being 'explained to people before I open my mouth' outside with being 'merely the man who takes a bica' there.
questions.hint: See his first response about being 'spared.'

questions.prompt: Which is the best paraphrase of 'Love and attention are not the same currency, though we spend them as if they were'?
options: Loving someone guarantees that you keep noticing them
We wrongly assume that loving someone means we still pay attention to them
Attention is more valuable than love
Love cannot be measured at all
questions.explanation: Tunde separates love from attention and says we treat them as identical when they are not — 'spend them as if they were.'
questions.hint: Focus on the line distinguishing two 'currencies.'

questions.prompt: What does Tunde mean by the grandmother example?
options: She could only read moods already filed away, not new ones
Elderly people lose the ability to read emotions
Inês never showed her grandmother any emotion
questions.explanation: He says she could read 'the moods you had already shown her' but not the mood 'you had never dared show,' because her image left 'no room for it.'
questions.hint: Read the exchange about the doorway and the 'file.'

questions.prompt: Which statement is Tunde NOT making?
options: Tenderness can also function as a way of not looking
We should refuse to know anyone well
Memory of a person can stop updating
questions.explanation: He explicitly denies this: 'I am not arguing against being known.' The other three are claims he affirms.
questions.hint: Check the response that starts 'There is everything to be said for it.'

questions.prompt: What does the metaphor of 'the file' convey?
options: People keep written records of their friends
A fixed mental image becomes harder to revise the closer it is
Strangers have no file on us
questions.explanation: 'The closer the file, the harder to revise' frames intimacy as a record that resists updating.
questions.hint: See the line ending 'no room for it.'

questions.prompt: How does Tunde describe the poured bica that awaited him?
options: As both a kindness and a small wall
questions.explanation: He says it 'is a kindness, and it is also a small wall. Both at once.'
questions.hint: Look near the phrase 'a tenderness that doubles.'

questions.prompt: What does Inês concede by the end?
options: That she will start watching people she assumed she understood
That she will stop serving regulars
questions.explanation: She says she'll 'start watching the people I am sure I already understand.'

questions.prompt: What does Tunde advise instead of watching people 'harder'?
options: Watching them as if he might be wrong
questions.explanation: 'Do not watch them harder. Watch them as if you might be wrong.'

questions.prompt: The phrase 'it accrues like interest' implies that people wrongly believe knowing someone…
options: Grows automatically with the passage of time
questions.explanation: Tunde mocks 'the lazy faith that being known... accrues like interest simply because the years pass.'

questions.prompt: Why does Tunde say his decision to 'take the bica sitting down' matters?
options: It enacts his point that he can still surprise her habitual picture of him
questions.explanation: Having argued that her image of him is frozen, he immediately contradicts the routine she'd filed — demonstrating revisability.
questions.hint: Connect his final action to the 'freezes it' argument.

questions.prompt: What tone best characterises the dialogue?
questions.explanation: Tunde provokes thought ('an uncomfortable morning') without hostility, and Inês engages openly.
questions.hint: Note 'I take no offence' and 'uncomfortable morning.'

questions.prompt: Which idea would Tunde most likely agree with?
options: A stranger can sometimes attend to us more truly than a loved one
Only people who love us can truly see us
Privacy is more important than connection
Old friends are always the most perceptive
questions.explanation: He claims to have been 'attended to, beautifully, by strangers who felt nothing.'

questions.prompt: What does Inês's remark that the theory is 'too neat' function to do?
options: Raise a reasonable objection that the argument is conveniently tidy
questions.explanation: She pushes back that the symmetry 'seems too neat, and a little cruel,' keeping the debate balanced.
questions.hint: Her line beginning 'So you are saying...'

questions.prompt: The overall argument turns on the paradox that…
options: Strangers are always better than friends
The very closeness that should help us see people can blind us to who they have become
Coffee shops are better than homes
questions.explanation: The dialogue's tension is that intimacy can freeze rather than refresh our perception of a familiar person.
questions.hint: Synthesise the 'freezes it' and 'stopped updating' lines.

questions.prompt: What does 'a tenderness that doubles as a way of not having to look again' suggest about kindness between familiar people?
options: An affectionate habit can quietly substitute for fresh attention
questions.explanation: Tunde says kindness can be 'both at once' — genuine and a wall that excuses us from looking afresh.

cloze.template:
Inês: You have been coming to this cafe for eleven years, Senhor Okafor, and I realised this morning that I could not tell you a single thing about your life beyond your coffee order.
Tunde: Which is precisely why I keep coming. What you call not knowing me, I would call being {{b1}}. Out there I am {{b2}} to people before I open my mouth; here I am merely the man who takes a bica standing up.
Inês: But does it not trouble you? We see each other more often than I see most of my friends, and yet I have, in a sense, stopped looking. You have become furniture to me, and I do not mean that unkindly.
Tunde: I take no offence. Furniture is loyal. The trouble, if there is one, is not that you have stopped looking but that you believe a longer look would have shown you more. Familiarity does not deepen the picture; it {{b3}} it. A glance held a moment too long would have served us both better than a thousand pleasant mornings.
Inês: That is a {{b4}} theory for a man who likes his privacy. Surely the people who have watched us for years know things our new acquaintances never will. My grandmother could read my mood from the doorway.
Tunde: She could read the moods you had already shown her. What she could not see was the mood you had never dared show, because the version of you she had filed away left no room for it. The closer the file, the harder to {{b5}}.
Inês: So you are saying that the people who recognise us are, in fact, the least equipped to notice when we change. That seems too {{b6}}, and a little cruel to the people who love us.
Tunde: Not cruel. Honest. Love and attention are not the same {{b7}}, though we spend them as if they were. I have been loved by people who had stopped attending to me years before, and attended to, beautifully, by strangers who felt nothing. I have watched widowers grieve a wife they had ceased, in any living sense, to perceive.
Inês: Then what would you have me do, refuse to know anyone well? I cannot serve a thousand strangers and call it {{b8}}. There is something to be said for the person who notices you have ordered nothing today.
Tunde: There is everything to be said for it. I am not arguing against being known; I am arguing against the lazy faith that being known is automatic, that it {{b9}} like interest simply because the years pass. It does not. It {{b10}} unless renewed.
Inês: Renewed how? I cannot interview my regulars each morning to check whether they have become someone new overnight.
Tunde: No, but you could resist the small {{b11}} that you already have my answer before I give it. The moment you finish my sentence for me, you have replaced me with your {{b12}} of me, and your memory, however affectionate, stopped updating long ago.
Inês: I confess I do that. I had your bica poured before you reached the counter. I thought it was a kindness.
Tunde: It is a kindness, and it is also a small {{b13}}. Both at once. Most of what passes between familiar people is exactly that: a tenderness that doubles as a way of not having to look again. We mistake the {{b14}} for closeness.
Inês: You have given me an uncomfortable morning, Senhor Okafor. I shall have to start {{b15}} the people I am sure I already understand.
Tunde: Do not watch them harder. Watch them as if you might be {{b16}}. That is the only recognition worth having, the kind that leaves the door open. And, since you are watching, I shall, today, take the bica sitting down.

blanks.explanation: 'being spared' means being mercifully exempted from being pre-judged.
He is 'explained to people' — interpreted for them in advance.
Familiarity 'freezes' the picture, fixing it in place.
Inês calls it 'a convenient theory' for a private man.
The closer the file, the harder to 'revise' it.
She says the theory seems 'too neat' — suspiciously tidy.
Love and attention are 'not the same currency.'
She cannot serve strangers and 'call it intimacy.'
He mocks the belief that being known 'accrues like interest.'
He urges resisting 'the small certainty' that you already have the answer.
You replace him with 'your memory' of him.
The kindness is also 'a small wall.'
She will start 'watching' people she assumed she understood.
Watch them as if you might be 'wrong.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Tunde claims that the people who know us best may be the least able to notice when we change. Think of someone you have known for many years: is your image of them still being updated, or did it quietly stop revising at some point — and what would it cost you to look at them 'as if you might be wrong'?
